channel.title: Group-Theory on Chen Kai Blog
channel.description: Recent content in Group-Theory on Chen Kai Blog

item.title: Homomorphisms, Normal Subgroups, and Quotient Groups
item.description:
Maps that respect structure#

A function between groups that preserves the operation is called a homomorphism. This is the right notion of “map” in group theory — it tells you when two groups are structurally related, even if their elements look completely different.

The definition#

A group homomorphism $\varphi: G \to H$ satisfies:

$$ \varphi(ab) = \varphi(a)\varphi(b) \quad \forall\, a, b \in G $$

Consequences: $\varphi(e_G) = e_H$ and $\varphi(a^{-1}) = \varphi(a)^{-1}$ .

item.title: Groups and Subgroups — the Algebra of Symmetry
item.description:
Why groups?#

Rotate a square 90 degrees. It looks the same. Flip it, then rotate — still the same square. These operations compose, they associate, and each one can be undone. That pattern — composition, associativity, invertibility — shows up everywhere: clock arithmetic, matrix multiplication, permutations of a Rubik’s cube, gauge transformations in physics.

A group strips away everything except the composition rule and asks: what can we deduce from these three axioms alone?
